content:
Would it be possible to mark functionality which is not allowed to be called from interrupt context with some kind of "noirq type"? And with this make the compiler complain if someone tries to compile code which is not supposed to be run in interrupt context into a interrupt context function? Similar to what Benno does with the untrusted data, but instead of marking data untrusted mark the functionality as "not allowed to be executed in irq context".

Background is https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/ZvwKTinnLckZm8aQ@boqun-archlinux/ where lockdep complains that a mutex is used in interrupt context. While lockdep catches this at runtime, I wonder if the Rust type system could somehow be used to catch this at compile time?

In this specific case I wonder if it would be possible to mark the timer callback as an "interrupt context function" and the mutex as "not allowed to be called from interrupt context". And with that make the compiler fail on it.

Would that be possible?
@Dirk Behme maybe analyzing the CFG to find instances of might_sleep()? I wonder if that could be implemented on klint.
